speaker: interviewer
text: Tape number 6. December 21, 1995. I am interviewing Ira Kaminsky. My name is Daniel Sedlis. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. Now, this was when I made matzah. I was very happy that the people were-- I made them happy, that they could observe for the first time after so many years of being in the ghetto and Skarzysko. And they couldn't-- they didn't observe any, any of the Jewish holidays. One guy-- this was interesting. One guy, he made HaMotzi, whatever he remembered. And the other guy said a prayer from, from the Seder, you know, the-- from the Pesach Seder, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. Or somebody said, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], from Pesach, you know. Everybody had certain different, though. Anyway, I was very happy that the people were happy. And they observed Pesach in a way they could observe the best in the concentration camp. Then a short time-- a short time after Pesach, I don't know. It was in the middle or end of April.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: They liquidated because the Russians in one side from east, and the American Army from, from the west side-- they already captured Buchenwald.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: April, I believe, I still remember. April 12th or 13th, Buchenwald was captured. And they were closing in where we were in Schlieben, the Russians and this. And they were evacuating us. And we were very lucky because they got some cattle cars for us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we didn't have the same-- the same-- the same suffering what the other camps had. Because at that time, they had the well-known 'Toten March.'

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thousands upon thousands of people, prisoners, mostly Jewish prisoners, they took them for a walk without food, without any, any surprise, in freezing, in cold weather. And most of them didn't survive the Toten March.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: As a matter of fact, we came after about a-- five or six days traveling in the-- in the cattle cars, they brought us to a place, Terezin-- Theresienstadt in Czechoslovakia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: We came to Theresienstadt. We were very surprised. People were dressed-- were dressed nice, clean. They looked good, not bad, not like us in our camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we didn't know what, what it means. What-- why-- how come there was a place like this in existence-- a camp like this, the concentration camp? We learned later on that this was the model camp that the SS, the German Nazis, created to show for the Red Cross and for anybody who was asking, who was brought-- who brought up that the Jewish people are not treated right, or they're getting killed.

speaker: interviewee
text: They said, come on, I'll show you. I have here a camp. And I'll show you how nice we are treating the Jewish people. And especially for the Red Cross, they showed off. And that's why they kept-- and people-- the living quarters were not bad. And the food was not bad. And there were no beating like at the other camps. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We were the first transport who came in from the people from the [NON-ENGLISH]. And we were happy, like I said, we came by trains. And they-- the way we were treated is properly. I would never forget. We had to go in to a-- take a shower and leave all our old clothes. They threw away. And they gave us, after the shower, new-- not new, but old, clean clothes. What they-- then there were-- they came. They started to come a lot of people from all over. In the thousands they came from the [NON-ENGLISH]. They came exhausted, sick, half-dead. And I volunteered at that time to work and receive all those sick people. And in the-- in that place where we washed them, we clothed them, and this. And I was helping them nice. In the meantime, and this was in April-- in April. And May 8th, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] about two weeks later after we came, the war ended. May 8th, the war ended. And we were very happy. We know that we survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: But not everybody was happy. Why? We-- you know, the Russians came in. The Russians liberated over there-- Theresienstadt. And we were very happy. They helped us also with food, with treatment for the sick-- the sick people. And after the war ended, a lot of people ran back wherever they came from, from Hungary, Romania, or, or Greece, or Poland, Germany.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I will never forget, and I would like to bring it up-- to bring it up for-- a fact that-- it's very nice. From Holland-- after the war ended, Holland sent a special train consisting from about between more than 20 cars, the trains, to pick up all the citizens of Holland and take them home. I was very, very surprised. And I would like to bring it up. It was a real nice gesture from the government of Holland to take care of their own Jewish people. And I-- as a matter of fact, I, I, I became very friendly with one redhead Jewish girl. And I was very, very sorry. She asked me if I would like to go to Holland, but I couldn't because I was waiting. I thought-- I was waiting for my sister to come. And I didn't go to Holland. And anyway, people from Holland went back nicely to their own-- their own country. And, and, and, and this-- over there, I was asking everybody when people came if they heard about my sister. I expected. And I knew that my sister, Rachel--

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: --that she survived. She was taken. When the-- when they were taking the men to Buchenwald, the women-- all the women from Skarzysko, from our Work C, they were taken to Leipzig. And the women, my sister included, were working in Leipzig. And I expected her also to come to us. And I didn't-- number one, they asked me and my brother if we would be nice-- so nice to stay and continue to work over there.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Russians asked you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not the Russians. They were a group of dedicated Jewish, Jewish doctors, medical doctors--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were--

speaker: interviewee
text: --who were mostly from Czechoslovakia and Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they asked-- they asked us-- we should stay and help them. Because everybody-- a lot of them were running away. And here, thousands of people kept coming in--

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: --in very bad shape. And they needed help. I decided-- I decided, OK. And we were working. And we helped a lot of people. And when we saw after a while that these people survived, that these people got well, you know, they had much better food. And they had enough food. But they-- the Russians brought in some food, you know. And we lived, you know, more or less not bad--

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: --comparing. But I would have to say that a lot of people after the liberation didn't live long enough, and they died. They, they, they started giving everybody food, and fat food. They wanted the people to gain weight--

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: --because we were skeletons-- came, you know. And these foods were not good for these people that they were starving for some times. On the [NON-ENGLISH], they didn't eat for sometimes. And a lot of them had diarrhea mostly. And when I saw that, I remember the Frankfurter Hall was full of them. And they died one after the other. And they're-- and they're hundreds, maybe thousands died after they were liberated. They died from eating, not from starvation. And they got sick. Anyway, my sister-- my sister didn't show up. And it was already after three months being over there in Theresienstadt. We decided, well, I have to go and look for our sister. We have to. Maybe she is in Poland. And we decided to go to Poland. And Poland-- we came to Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get there?

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get there?

speaker: interviewee
text: By train-- trains. We had to pay.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you have the money?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we, we, we organized something, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we had some money. And we, we sold something, you know. You know, people brought something.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And, and we had something. And we could go-- we could go for nothing, without paying, even Czechoslovakia. Let me tell something. The Czechish people behaved towards us. They knew that we are not from Czechoslovakia, that we were from Poland. But I could understand a little Czech because from Polish the Czech is a little similar. They behaved very nice. They went out of their way to help us, also, the Czech people. As a matter of fact, I met over there-- President Benes came to visit us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was President at that time. He came back from England. And he took over the leadership of this for a while in Czechoslovakia. And then we went to Poland. We went to Czestochowa first with a friend. We didn't have anybody. We didn't know where to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: So a friend-- my-- a friend went, got the word that in Czestochowa, his two sisters survived. I said, well, OK. There are a lot of Jewish people over there. So we went to Czestochowa. And I was looking. I was a few days over there. And I was looking. I couldn't find my sister.

speaker: interviewee
text: They told me another place where she might be was Lodz. Lodz-- a lot of Jewish people were concentrated over there. And a, a Jewish committee was formed over there. And the Jewish people in Lodz had a better chance to, to live over there. But the word went around-- don't go to the small places home because it's very dangerous. There is an organization, anti-Semitic organization, that they are against the communist regime. And they are-- at the same time, they are also-- they are considering the Jews as, as communists. And they were killing wherever they can. They're against the Russians and against the Jews. And they were killing a lot of Jews. We should watch out. Anyway, we decided. And we went to Lodz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And in Lodz were a lot of people, a lot of Jewish people. In Lodz there were more liberal type of people, even the Polish people more liberal than in the countryside. And we were over there. And we were looking for my sister. In the meantime, I I had to organize something. We, we found a room. And in our room, we, we found a big, big room over there without windows, And we settled over there. And we took in everybody whoever came from my city or some friend, well, he came to, to, to us. And he had a place. Even one guy who, who was-- at that time, I, I, I made already some money. At that time, a, a guy came, a captain for the Polish Army. A friend of ours from our city-- he was a young man. And he was in the army. And they sent him, the Russians-- and the-- and the Polish Army which was created in Russia-- they sent him to a school. And he became a captain. He came straight out from school. He didn't have a single penny. I said, come on to my house and stay with me. Anybody who ever came, they stayed with us. And I made already, like I said, a little money. And, and they had food until they helped themselves. They stayed with us. We were interested to find our sister. We couldn't find her. Once we were on the street, we found somebody from Work C, a woman. And she lived over there.

speaker: interviewer
text: How are you, and all these things. She says, how, how is every-- I said, we-- no good. It's no good. We are here already. We are looking for sometimes we can't find our sister, Rachel. She said, you don't know where she is? Of course I don't know.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, she found out that my sister is with another group of Jewish-- of Jewish women, girls, in a place where they can't leave. They are locked up-- not in a camp, but they are not allowed to leave. The Russians are over there. This is a factory of the vodka. And they wouldn't let them go. They don't pay them, but they wouldn't let them go. We found out where it is. And my brother took upon himself-- my brother, Harry, took some money, and he took also a golden ring in case if we have to bribe somebody. And that's what he did. He found the place where the woman told us to find my sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: There was already over there about another six or eight more Jewish young girls. And he came to-- they should release my sister. They said, no, she can't go. He said, what do you mean, she can't go? The war is over. Why didn't you let her go? She can't go. I had an order to keep them. And that-- that's what I'm going to do.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, my brother bribed him with golden ring and some money. And he said, I can't let her go now, but in a few days, she will go home. And that's the way it was. After a few days, my sister came to Lodz. We left her the-- this-- we left her the address. And she came. And we were at least together with my sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right after my brother came back, we went, both of us, to the Jewish committee in Lodz. And we notified them. Here, the war is over. I came, you know, with my brother. And here, the Jewish girls are still prisoners in this and this place. How come? You have to see immediately to release these prisoners over there.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was already three, four-- three, four months after the war they kept them over there. Later, I found out that they were not the only ones. There were more Jewish girls that they were kept like that. Anyway, this-- that Russian who was in charge of the factory over there-- of the vodka factory, realized if we will come over there and if my sister is released, that we will notify them. And while I was doing some business on the trains going somewhere-- and I saw six girls over there. And my brother knew them already from visiting my sister. And he said, did they-- and they, they released us. They said, but we don't have where to go. He said, go. My sister is home. You, you know her. We gave them the address. And all the girls stayed in our place, too, when they were released over there. Anyway, as soon as we had my sister, we were thinking not to stay in Poland because we couldn't stay in Poland. My brother had a very bad experience. He went to our town, our city, Chmielnik. And he-- for some reason, the Polish-- it was one day. The-- a few Polish men came to him. And they said to him, when it will get dark, you have to leave. And if you're not going to leave, we aren't responsible for what will happen to you. He saw already what was going on. And he left right away. My brother didn't tell me that he had that experience. After a week, I was parked. And I wanted to go home also to Chmielnik. I want to see my town where I was born. Maybe I'll find some friends or somebody. I was looking also-- I thought maybe a little brother will be alive. Because my brother, Mendel, at 11-- when he was 11 and 1/2 years old, he went to work for a Polish farmer. And the Polish farmer gave him food. And even he brought some bread from the Polish farmer from the work that he did. He didn't pay him money, but with bread. And he brought home. And it helped us, my mother, and the other children, and-- to support. And a little boy of 11 1/2 years. He wasn't even 12 years at that time. We thought maybe he was hiding over there at the farm. Or maybe he's still alive. But to no avail. He was not alive. We found out that he went-- later on, we found out that he went also to Treblinka. And he was--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you find out?

speaker: interviewee
text: From people-- that they were-- they were people that they, they went with the liquidation, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And on the way, they escaped.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: A friend of mine, he was a second cousin.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH]. He saw our family when we were taken. This was-- about, about six kilometer after the city, they stopped. And all the people-- and they saw who was with them, you know. And he told me that Mendel was also with my family when they were taking them to, to Treblinka.

speaker: interviewer
text: Anyway, I wanted to go. And my brother says, you're not going to go. I said, what do you mean I'm not going to go? See, he didn't tell me what happened to him, that they were threatening him and his life. Then you're not going to go.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, I have to go. And I'm ready, I'm taking everything. I had a satchel with me, and everything else. I have to go. Over my dead body, he said. Well, then I saw already that-- something serious. Then he told me that they were threatening his life, the Polish. And if you come over there-- and they know you very well, the Polish people over there. We were well-known over there from school, and from this, and everything. My brother was working over there. From the barbershop, they know him and everything. You're not going to go. Anyway, I didn't go because of-- my brother said, it's very dangerous for us. We didn't go. Anyway, we decided that we have to leave and go to-- we have to leave. We have a sister in Israel. We-- I finally I found-- at that time, I found her whereabouts, you know, in Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Palestine?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, in Palestine. And I, I wrote a letter. And she received the letter. And I said, we have to go-- leave Poland because there was no life. There was no future in Poland at that time. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So we decided--

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were in Lodz at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: We decided to leave Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: From Lodz?

speaker: interviewee
text: From Lodz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: So a friend of my-- a friend of mine who lived over there, a neighbor, he said, if you leave Poland and if you go to Austria or to Germany, you've got to have marks. Marks, with the German--

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm, currency.

speaker: interviewee
text: --German marks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: German currency. So I said, I don't have any marks. He said, don't worry. I'll get you some marks. OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: So once, he came in. And he said, OK, Kaminsky, I've got somebody who has marks for you. I says, who is this? This man has marks for you. He said, how many do you have? I said, what, I don't know. 10,000 marks or something. I said, how much is it? So he told me a certain amount of Polish money. And I took, and I paid him. And as soon as he got the Polish money, he put it into his pocket. And he took out one side a revolver, and one side a, a, a this, showing me that he is from the UB. You call it the UB, [NON-ENGLISH]. It's from the-- from the-- from the police. Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And you are under arrest. Me and my brother. They were accusing us that we were dealing with marks and money.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, I don't have any anymore, the, the new-- the new money. You can check. And they were looking all over. They took the money. And also, my sister when she was-- I mentioned that she was working in the-- in the vodka factory. She didn't get paid money. But she got-- when she left, they figured out how much they owed her. So they said, we owe you about 15-- what do they have-- liters, I think, over there, vodka.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she came with 15 liters of vodka. They took also the 15 liters of vodka. They thought that I'm-- that we are doing business with vodka, but we didn't. It was her, her vodka. She told them, here, I've got to show you. Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's where I was working. And this was the vodka that I got paid for my work for many-- for several months. They took everything. And they locked us up. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My sister went-- terrible, aggravated, for nothing. Well, and they locked us up, the police, in, in, in this-- in prison. But we-- she came-- that she had to look for that captain that I mentioned before. He was a captain in the Polish Army. From the police-- also, he was assigned to be a police-- and work in the police, in the Polish police, as a captain.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you have to help me because my brothers, my two brothers, are in arrest. They were-- they are in prison. And he went up-- he was a captain. He went up to the police. And he said, how come you arrested my two cousins? And I was not a cousin. But I was a good friend. I helped him out. Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: When he came from school, he didn't have a single penny.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: He wanted to go out with a girl. I gave him for cigarettes. And I gave him some money he should have to take out the girl, and for himself for spending money. He says, they were tremendous. He told my sister, your brothers behaved towards me. They helped me very-- so much--

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: --I have to do something.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH]. [NON-ENGLISH] [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was-- he is still alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: But he was-- he is a sick man.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he went in to the police, you arrested my two-- my two cousins? How come? They didn't do anything. They didn't do anything wrong.

speaker: interviewer
text: So over there, he was a lieutenant-- Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: --in charge of the police. He saluted to the captain, you know. He says, I didn't arrest your cousins. Who are your cousins? Yes, you do have. The two Kaminskys are my cousins. I want you to release them, to let them go. OK, go ahead and take them. And he took us out from prison.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where is the vodka? You took about 15 liters. [LAUGHS] There were two left. So the captain, my friend, took to this-- took the, the two bottles of vodka for himself.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we decided we had to leave Poland. Poland is not for us. We left Poland with the Bricha.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is illegal. We went as Greek Jews going back to Greek. Our, our language had to be only Hebrew. And I knew a little Hebrew, you know? And I had a group, about 25 people, with me. We went through Czechoslovakia. When we were in Czechoslovakia, we still had to be playing the game as Greek returning to Greece. And from over there, we went to Austria, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Austria. We came to Linz, Oberosterreich Linz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time, they opened a new place for the displaced people, Displaced Persons camp, in [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. In [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], they assigned a room for us. This was an apartment. It was three little rooms. They put in three families, OK? And, and we were over there. It wasn't, wasn't bad. And I was glad that I'm out. It was a relief that we went when we left the communist countries, that time we were in Poland, Czechoslovakia. And when we came to the American zone, we really felt a big relief.

speaker: interviewer
text: Anyway, I was over there a couple of days only. A, a delegation of three men came to me. Are you Israel Kaminsky? I said, yes. Are you Madrich Kaminsky? I looked at them. Yes, how do you know about me? I just came. I, I, I didn't see anybody. And I thought nobody knows that I am here. How do you know? We found out that you are here. And 'madrich' means instructor.

speaker: interviewee
text: Instructor. 'Madrich' means instructor. Are you Instructor Kaminsky? I said, yes. How do you know? We found out. Where-- I still don't know how they found out. We would like you to come. And they told me, here, in the American zone, the American administration encouraged [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the creation-- I should say, a recreation of political parties in-- over there in the camp. They wanted people should be-- you know, they, they, they, they should know where they-- where they-- where they belong. OK. Thank you. Now let's--
